source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
title: Ensure GKE clusters are not running using the Compute Engine default service account

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
input_row.title: Ensure GKE clusters are not running using the Compute Engine default service account
input_row.description: Create and use minimally privileged Service accounts to run GKE cluster nodes instead of using the Compute Engine default Service account. Unnecessary permissions could be abused in the case of a node compromise.
input_row.rationale: A GCP service account (as distinct from a Kubernetes ServiceAccount) is an identity that an instance or an application can be used to run GCP API requests. This identity is used to identify virtual machine instances to other Google Cloud Platform services. By default, Kubernetes Engine nodes use the Compute Engine default service account. This account has broad access by default, as defined by access scopes, making it useful to a wide variety of applications on the VM, but it has more permissions than are required to run your Kubernetes Engine cluster. A minimally privileged service account should be created and used to run the Kubernetes Engine cluster instead of using the Compute Engine default service account, and create separate service accounts for each Kubernetes Workload (See recommendation 5.2.2). Kubernetes Engine requires, at a minimum, the node service account to have the monitoring.viewer, monitoring.metricWriter, and logging.logWriter roles. Additional roles may need to be added for the nodes to pull images from GCR. Impact: Instances are automatically granted the https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud- platform scope to allow full access to all Google Cloud APIs. This is so that the IAM permissions of the instance are completely determined by the IAM roles of the Service account. Thus if Kubernetes workloads were using cluster access scopes to perform actions using Google APIs, they may no longer be able to, if not permitted by the permissions of the Service account. To remediate, follow recommendation 5.2.2. The Service account roles listed here are the minimum required to run the cluster. Additional roles may be required to pull from a private instance of Google Container Registry (GCR).
input_row.audit: Using Google Cloud Console: 1. Go to Kubernetes Engine by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list 2. Select the cluster under test and click on each Node pool to bring up the Node pool details page. Ensure that for each Node pool the Service account is not set to default under the Security heading. To check the permissions allocated to the service account are the minimum required for cluster operation: 1. Go to IAM by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/iam-admin/iam 2. From the list of Service accounts, ensure each cluster Service account has only the following roles: • Logs Writer • Monitoring Metric Writer • Monitoring Viewer Using Command line: To check which Service account is set for an existing cluster, run the following command: gcloud container node-pools describe $NODE_POOL --cluster $CLUSTER_NAME -- zone $COMPUTE_ZONE --format json | jq '.config.serviceAccount' The output of the above command will return default if default Service account is used for Project access. To check that the permissions allocated to the service account are the minimum required for cluster operation: gcloud projects get-iam-policy <project_id> \ --flatten="bindings[].members" \ --format='table(bindings.role)' \ --filter="bindings.members:<service_account>" Review the output to ensure that the service account only has the roles required to run the cluster: • roles/logging.logWriter • roles/monitoring.metricWriter • roles/monitoring.viewer
input_row.remediation: Using Google Cloud Console: To create a minimally privileged service account: 1. Go to Service Accounts by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/iam- admin/serviceaccounts. 2. Click on CREATE SERVICE ACCOUNT. 3. Enter Service Account Details. 4. Click CREATE AND CONTINUE. 5. Within Service Account permissions add the following roles: o Logs Writer. o Monitoring Metric Writer. o `Monitoring Viewer. 6. Click CONTINUE. 7. Grant users access to this service account and create keys as required. 8. Click DONE. To create a Node pool to use the Service account: 1. Go to Kubernetes Engine by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list. 2. Click on the cluster name within which the Node pool will be launched. 3. Click on ADD NODE POOL. 4. Within the Node Pool details, select the Security subheading, and under `Identity defaults, select the minimally privileged service account from the Service Account drop-down. 5. Click `CREATE to launch the Node pool. Note: The workloads will need to be migrated to the new Node pool, and the old node pools that use the default service account should be deleted to complete the remediation. Using Command Line: To create a minimally privileged service account: gcloud iam service-accounts create <node_sa_name> --display-name "GKE Node Service Account" export NODE_SA_EMAIL=gcloud iam service-accounts list --format='value(email)' --filter='displayName:GKE Node Service Account' Grant the following roles to the service account: export PROJECT_ID=gcloud config get-value project gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding <project_id> --member serviceAccount:<node_sa_email> --role roles/monitoring.metricWriter gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding <project_id> --member serviceAccount:<node_sa_email> --role roles/monitoring.viewer gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding <project_id> --member serviceAccount:<node_sa_email> --role roles/logging.logWriter To create a new Node pool using the Service account, run the following command: gcloud container node-pools create <node_pool> --service- account=<sa_name>@<project_id>.iam.gserviceaccount.com-- cluster=<cluster_name> --zone <compute_zone> Note: The workloads will need to be migrated to the new Node pool, and the old node pools that use the default service account should be deleted to complete the remediation. Default Value: By default, nodes use the Compute Engine default service account when you create a new cluster. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/access/service- accounts#compute_engine_default_service_account
input_row.impact: Instances are automatically granted the https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud- platform scope to allow full access to all Google Cloud APIs. This is so that the IAM permissions of the instance are completely determined by the IAM roles of the Service account. Thus if Kubernetes workloads were using cluster access scopes to perform actions using Google APIs, they may no longer be able to, if not permitted by the permissions of the Service account. To remediate, follow recommendation 5.2.2. The Service account roles listed here are the minimum required to run the cluster. Additional roles may be required to pull from a private instance of Google Container Registry (GCR).

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The compliance control can be verified through the use of GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud). The audit process as described involves checking the service account used for GKE node pools and ensuring it is not the default Compute Engine service account. This can be performed programmatically using the provided `gcloud` commands, which allows for automation of this verification process.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_gke_node_pool_service_account_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use `gcloud container node-pools describe` to retrieve details about the node pools in a GKE cluster.
- Check the `config.serviceAccount` field to ensure it is not set to the default Compute Engine service account (indicated by 'default').
- Use `gcloud projects get-iam-policy` to review the IAM policy of the service account associated with each node pool.
- Ensure that the service account only has the necessary roles: `roles/logging.logWriter`, `roles/monitoring.metricWriter`, and `roles/monitoring.viewer`.
- Pass criteria: The node pool does not use the default service account and the associated service account has only the minimal required roles.
- This approach involves listing and examining GKE node pools and their respective configurations, and verifying the IAM roles assigned to the service account.
- This verification can be automated across all clusters and node pools within a GCP project for compliance auditing purposes.
step2_review:
---

## 1. APPROACH VALIDATION: **AGREE**

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:**  
  The classification as **Automated** is correct. This control can be programmatically validated using GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud`). There is no need for manual inspection; all required information is accessible via CLI or API.

- **GCP SDK/CLI Capabilities:**  
  - `gcloud container node-pools describe` and `gcloud container clusters list` can enumerate node pools and their configuration, including the service account used.
  - Service account IAM roles can be checked with `gcloud projects get-iam-policy` or `gcloud iam service-accounts get-iam-policy`.
  - No need for CloudFormation (AWS-specific) or GCP Config (Config Validator could be used as an alternative, but not required for automation).

---

## 2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY: **Score: 8/10**

- **Strengths:**
  - Correctly identifies the use of `gcloud container node-pools describe` to extract the `config.serviceAccount` field.
  - Correctly identifies the need to check IAM roles for the service account.
  - Automation details are generally accurate and feasible.

- **Areas for Improvement:**
  - The default Compute Engine service account is not simply indicated by 'default' in the `config.serviceAccount` field. Instead, it is typically of the form:  
    `PROJECT_NUMBER-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com`
    The check should explicitly match this pattern, not just look for the word 'default'.
  - The use of `gcloud projects get-iam-policy` is overly broad. To check the roles assigned to a specific service account, use:  
    `gcloud iam service-accounts get-iam-policy <SERVICE_ACCOUNT_EMAIL>`
  - The program name (`gcp_gke_node_pool_service_account_check`) is descriptive but could be more specific, e.g., `gcp_gke_node_pool_non_default_service_account_check`.
  - The check for "only the minimal required roles" is good, but the process for verifying **only** those roles (and not more) should be clarified (e.g., ensure no additional roles are present).

---

## 3. IMPROVEMENTS

- **Technical Improvements:**
  - **Default Service Account Detection:**  
    Instead of checking for 'default', match the actual default Compute Engine service account pattern:  
    `PROJECT_NUMBER-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com`
  - **IAM Role Verification:**  
    Use `gcloud iam service-accounts get-iam-policy` for precise role bindings on the service account.
  - **Role Minimization:**  
    Explicitly check that the service account has **no roles except** the minimal required set.
  - **Alternative Methods:**  
    - Consider using GCP Config Validator or Forseti for policy-as-code enforcement.
    - The GCP Asset Inventory API can also be used for large-scale audits.
  - **Program Name Suggestion:**  
    `gcp_gke_node_pool_non_default_service_account_minimal_roles_check`  
    (More explicit about both the non-default account and minimal roles requirement.)

- **Missing Considerations:**
  - The process should account for clusters with multiple node pools, each potentially using different service accounts.
  - The control should clarify whether **all** node pools must comply or if exceptions are allowed.
  - Consider automation for multi-project environments.

---

## 4. CONFIDENCE: **HIGH**

- The assessment is fundamentally sound and technically feasible with GCP APIs and SDKs.
- Minor technical clarifications are needed for precise and robust automation.

---

## 5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION: **Modify**

- **Modify** the automation details to:
  - Explicitly check for the default Compute Engine service account by matching the actual service account email pattern.
  - Use `gcloud iam service-accounts get-iam-policy` for role verification.
  - Ensure the program name clearly reflects both the non-default service account and minimal roles check.
- The overall approach remains Automated, but technical precision and clarity should be improved for robust compliance.

---

**Summary Table:**

| Section                | Assessment                |
|------------------------|---------------------------|
| Approach Validation    | AGREE                     |
| Technical Accuracy     | 8/10                      |
| Improvements           | See above                 |
| Confidence             | HIGH                      |
| Final Recommendation   | Modify (see above)        |

---

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identified that this control can be fully validated using GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud`). The service account assigned to each GKE node pool is exposed via the `config.serviceAccount` field, and the default Compute Engine service account follows a predictable naming convention (`PROJECT_NUMBER-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com`). IAM roles for the service account can also be programmatically checked. No manual inspection is required, and all necessary properties are accessible via CLI/API.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
gcp_gke_node_pool_service_account_minimal_privilege_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **GCP Resource/Service:** Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) Node Pools, IAM Service Accounts
- **API/CLI Method:**  
  - `gcloud container clusters list` (to enumerate clusters)  
  - `gcloud container node-pools list --cluster=<cluster>` (to enumerate node pools)  
  - `gcloud container node-pools describe <node-pool> --cluster=<cluster>` (to get `config.serviceAccount`)  
  - `gcloud iam service-accounts get-iam-policy <service-account>` (to check assigned roles)
- **Properties to Check:**  
  - `config.serviceAccount` for each node pool: Must NOT be `PROJECT_NUMBER-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com` (the default Compute Engine service account).
  - IAM roles assigned to the service account: Must be limited to `roles/logging.logWriter`, `roles/monitoring.metricWriter`, and `roles/monitoring.viewer` (minimal required roles).
- **Pass Criteria:**  
  - No node pool in any cluster uses the default Compute Engine service account.
  - The service account assigned to each node pool has only the minimal required roles.
- **Fail Criteria:**  
  - Any node pool uses the default Compute Engine service account.
  - The assigned service account has excessive permissions beyond the minimal required roles.

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
Both AI opinions agreed on automation. The second opinion correctly clarified that the default service account is not simply labeled "default" but follows a specific naming pattern. This has been incorporated into the automation details for technical accuracy.
